title: Best Practices
subtitle: Write Angular code that scales, performs, and teams can maintain
description: The patterns, architecture decisions, and anti-patterns that separate production Angular apps from tutorial apps. Covers project structure, performance, security, TypeScript discipline, and testing fundamentals.
keyPoints: Use feature-based folder structure — group by feature, not by type (components/, services/)
Smart (container) components manage state and services; Dumb (presentational) components only render inputs and emit outputs
OnPush change detection reduces unnecessary re-renders — use it on every presentational component
Never call functions in templates — use pure pipes or computed signals instead
Lazy load every feature route to keep the initial bundle small
Use strict TypeScript — avoid any, prefer interfaces over classes for data shapes
Use trackBy in @for loops for large lists to prevent unnecessary DOM re-creation

sections.title: Project Structure — Feature-Based, Not Type-Based
sections.content:
The most important architectural decision in an Angular app is how you organise your folders. Two common approaches:

Type-based (avoid):
src/app/components/ — ALL components from every feature mixed together
src/app/services/   — ALL services
src/app/models/     — ALL interfaces

Problems: searching for a feature means jumping between 4+ folders. Impossible to tell which pieces belong together. Refactoring one feature touches everything.

Feature-based (recommended):
src/app/features/auth/          — login, register, auth guard, auth service
src/app/features/dashboard/     — dashboard component, dashboard service, widgets
src/app/features/products/      — list, detail, edit, product service, product model
src/app/core/                   — singleton services (auth, theme, http interceptors), layout
src/app/shared/                 — reusable components, pipes, directives used by 2+ features

Benefits: a new developer can understand the scope of a feature immediately. Lazy loading aligns naturally with feature folders. Deleting a feature means deleting one folder.

sections.caption: Dependencies flow in one direction: features can import from shared and core, but core and shared never import from features. This prevents circular dependencies and makes features independently deployable.

sections.title: Smart vs Dumb Components — The Container Pattern
sections.content:
The single most impactful architectural pattern for Angular component design:

Smart (Container) Components:
• Know about services, the router, and application state
• Inject services, make HTTP calls, read from stores
• Pass data DOWN to dumb components via inputs
• Listen to events from dumb components via outputs
• Usually correspond to a route
• NOT reusable — they are page-specific
• Examples: ProductListPageComponent, CheckoutPageComponent, DashboardComponent

Dumb (Presentational) Components:
• Receive data only via @Input() / input()
• Communicate only via @Output() / output()
• Know NOTHING about services, routing, or global state
• Purely a function of their inputs: same inputs always produce the same rendering
• Highly reusable and easy to test
• Can use OnPush change detection safely
• Examples: ProductCardComponent, PaginatorComponent, DataTableComponent, ButtonComponent

Why this matters: when a dumb component breaks, you know the problem is in its inputs or its template — not somewhere in a service chain. You can test it by simply passing mock inputs.

sections.title: Smart vs Dumb Component Pattern
sections.explanation: The dumb ProductCardComponent knows nothing about ProductService — it just renders a product object and emits a click event. You can test it by passing any object that matches the Product interface. The smart page component wires them together but has no HTML of its own to test.

sections.title: OnPush Change Detection — Boost Performance
sections.content:
By default, Angular uses Default change detection: after any async event anywhere in the app, Angular walks the entire component tree top-to-bottom, checking every component for changes. This is safe but wasteful.

OnPush change detection tells Angular: only re-check this component if:
1. One of its @Input() / input() references changes (object/array identity, not deep equality)
2. An event fires from inside this component (click, keydown, etc.)
3. An Observable bound with the async pipe emits a new value
4. A Signal it reads changes (Angular's future default)
5. You manually call markForCheck() or ChangeDetectorRef.detectChanges()

Result: Angular skips the component on most change detection cycles, checking it only when data it owns actually changes. For a component tree with 100 components, this can reduce CD work by 90%.

When to use OnPush: on ALL presentational/dumb components, and on any component that only receives data via inputs. Do NOT use it on components that mutate objects without creating new references — OnPush won't detect those mutations.

sections.title: OnPush Change Detection
sections.explanation: With modern Angular Signals, OnPush just works without any special handling — Signal reads in templates automatically schedule updates. This is one reason the Angular team is moving toward Signals as the default reactivity model.

sections.title: Performance — Pipes Over Functions, trackBy
sections.content: Two of the most common performance anti-patterns in Angular templates: 1. Calling functions in templates: • Every change detection cycle calls ALL functions in ALL visible templates • A function that filters a 1000-item array is called dozens of times per second on every mouse move, keypress, and setTimeout • The fix: use a pure pipe (calculated once, cached until inputs change) or a computed() signal (same caching behaviour) 2. Not using trackBy / track in @for loops: • Without track, Angular cannot tell if a list item moved, was updated, or was replaced • Angular destroys and recreates every DOM node in the list on every data change • With track item.id, Angular identifies which DOM nodes correspond to which data objects and only updates the ones that changed • For a list of 100 items where only 1 changed: without track = 100 DOM operations, with track = 1 DOM operation Other performance tips: • Lazy load images: loading="lazy" on tags • Virtual scrolling for long lists: @angular/cdk ScrollingModule • Avoid deeply nested component trees — flatten where possible • Use the Angular DevTools Chrome extension to profile change detection

sections.title: Pure Pipes and track — Two Essential Performance Fixes
sections.explanation: Pure pipes are memoised — Angular caches the result and only re-executes the pipe when its inputs change. track p.id in @for makes Angular match DOM nodes to data by id, so changing one item in a list of 1000 only updates one DOM node. These two changes can dramatically improve rendering performance in data-heavy apps.

sections.title: TypeScript Best Practices — Strict Mode and Type Safety
sections.content:
Angular apps are TypeScript — use TypeScript fully, not just as 'JavaScript with annotations'.

Enable strict mode in tsconfig.json:
• strict: true — enables strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, and more
• Without strictNullChecks, you cannot distinguish null, undefined, and a real value — you will have runtime null errors that TypeScript could have caught

Golden rules:
1. Never use any — it is a type-safety hole that defeats the entire purpose of TypeScript. Use unknown for truly unknown data (then narrow with type guards), or proper interfaces/generics.

2. Use interfaces for data shapes, classes only for objects with behaviour — API responses, state objects, config objects should all be interfaces, not classes.

3. Use readonly on inputs and injected services — it communicates intent and prevents accidental reassignment: private readonly http = inject(HttpClient).

4. Prefer explicit return types on public methods — this documents the API and catches return type mismatches at authoring time.

5. Use TypeScript discriminated unions for state modelling instead of multiple boolean flags: type State = { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'success'; data: User } | { status: 'error'; message: string }.

sections.title: TypeScript Patterns — Interfaces, Generics, Discriminated Unions
sections.explanation: Discriminated unions with a shared status field let TypeScript narrow the type in switch/if branches. In the 'success' branch, TypeScript knows data exists and is type T. In the 'error' branch, it knows message exists. This eliminates null checks and makes impossible states unrepresentable.

sections.title: Security — XSS Prevention and Safe HTML
sections.content:
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is the most common web security vulnerability. Angular has robust built-in protections, but you can inadvertently bypass them.

Angular's automatic protections:
• All {{ interpolation }} and [property] bindings are automatically sanitised — Angular escapes HTML characters
• Angular will NEVER execute a <script> tag injected via data binding

Where you can accidentally create XSS vulnerabilities:
1. [innerHTML]="userContent" — injects HTML. Angular sanitises it (removes script tags and event handlers) but it is still risky if content comes from user input.
2. DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml() — COMPLETELY disables sanitisation for that value. Only use for content YOU control (e.g. your own SVG files). Never for user-generated content.
3. Using document.write() or element.innerHTML directly in vanilla JS outside Angular

Additional security rules:
• Validate and sanitise all user input on the SERVER — client-side validation is UX, not security
• Use HttpClient — it automatically prevents JSON hijacking
• Store tokens in memory or HttpOnly cookies — NOT localStorage (vulnerable to XSS)
• Set Content-Security-Policy headers on your server to prevent inline script execution
• Keep dependencies updated — run npm audit regularly

sections.title: Security — Safe HTML Handling
sections.explanation: Angular's [innerHTML] sanitisation is good but not a reason to be careless. The rule: if content comes from a user or a third party, use interpolation {{ }} (always escaped) or let Angular sanitise [innerHTML]. Only call bypassSecurityTrustHtml() for SVGs and HTML you wrote yourself.

sections.title: Lazy Loading and Code Splitting
sections.content:
Every Angular app ships a main JavaScript bundle. Without lazy loading, EVERY component, service, and dependency is bundled into main.js — the user must download all of it before seeing the first screen.

Lazy loading splits the app into chunks that are downloaded only when needed:
• The initial bundle contains only what is needed for the first page
• Feature modules are downloaded when the user navigates to that route
• A large dashboard app might split into 10+ chunks — users who never visit the Admin section never download admin code

Modern Angular pattern: loadComponent() with standalone components — the simplest possible lazy loading, one line per route.

Guiding principles:
• Every route should use loadComponent() or loadChildren()
• Never import a feature component directly in the router file — this defeats lazy loading
• Use the Angular CLI's --lazy flag: ng generate component feature/my-feature --skip-import
• Analyse your bundle with: npx ng build --stats-json, then upload stats.json to webpack-bundle-analyzer

sections.title: Lazy Loading Routes and Preloading Strategy
sections.explanation: With loadComponent(), each import() call creates a separate JavaScript chunk. Angular and webpack/esbuild automatically figure out which code belongs in each chunk. The key rule: never import feature component classes directly at the top of a routes file — use dynamic import() inside loadComponent().

sections.title: Code Quality — Naming, Single Responsibility, Small Components
sections.content:
Consistent naming and focused components make a codebase navigable by anyone, not just the author.

Angular naming conventions:
• Components:  ProductListComponent     → product-list.component.ts
• Services:    ProductService           → product.service.ts
• Guards:      authGuard                → auth.guard.ts
• Interceptors: authInterceptor         → auth.interceptor.ts
• Pipes:       TruncatePipe             → truncate.pipe.ts
• Interfaces:  Product                  → product.model.ts or product.interface.ts
• Directives:  ClickOutsideDirective    → click-outside.directive.ts

Single Responsibility Principle:
• A component should do ONE thing. If you are writing component logic that touches more than 2 concerns, split it.
• Fat component symptoms: more than 300 lines, multiple unrelated methods, injecting 5+ services
• Extract repeated template blocks into sub-components
• Move business logic to services — components should orchestrate, not compute

Small functions:
• Functions longer than 20 lines almost always do too much
• Each function should have a single, clear name that explains exactly what it does
• Avoid boolean parameters (doSomething(true)) — create two separate functions instead
• Avoid deep nesting (if inside if inside if) — use early returns

sections.title: Naming Conventions and Clean Code Patterns
sections.explanation: Notice: readonly on injected services and exposed signals, early returns instead of nested if blocks, descriptive names that explain intent (inStockOnly not filtered, clearSelection not setNull), and small single-purpose private helpers. The public API surface is minimal — only what consumers need.

sections.content: Run ng lint after enabling the Angular ESLint schematic (ng add @angular-eslint/schematics). It catches common Angular anti-patterns automatically: missing trackBy, subscribing in constructors, direct DOM manipulation, missing standalone: true, and many more. Run it in CI to prevent regressions.

Use the Angular DevTools extension for Chrome and Firefox (search 'Angular DevTools' in the extension store). It shows you the component tree, each component's change detection cycles, injected dependencies, and Signal values in real time. The Profiler tab shows which components are taking the longest to render — invaluable for diagnosing performance issues.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between Smart and Dumb (Presentational) components?
interviewQuestions.a: Smart (Container) components know about services, routing, and application state. They inject services, orchestrate data flow, and pass data down to child components via inputs. They are page-specific and not reusable. Dumb (Presentational) components only receive data via @Input() / input() and communicate outward only via @Output() / output(). They know nothing about services, routing, or global state — they are purely a function of their inputs. Benefits: dumb components are highly reusable, trivial to unit test (just pass mock inputs), and safe to use with OnPush change detection.

interviewQuestions.q: What is OnPush change detection and when should you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: With Default change detection, Angular checks every component after every async event. With OnPush, Angular only checks a component when: one of its @Input() / input() references changes (new object reference), an event fires from inside the component, an Observable bound via async pipe emits, a Signal it reads changes, or markForCheck() is called manually. Use OnPush on all presentational/dumb components. The critical pitfall: OnPush won't detect mutations to existing objects — you must always create new object references (spread operator) when updating inputs. With Signals, OnPush works automatically without any special handling.

interviewQuestions.q: Why should you never call functions in Angular templates?
interviewQuestions.a: Angular's change detection calls every expression in every visible template on every CD cycle — which can be dozens of times per second. A function that filters a 1000-item array gets called every time the user moves the mouse. The fix: use pure pipes (Angular caches the result and only re-executes when the pipe's input changes) or computed() signals (memoised, only recalculate when dependencies change). Both approaches reduce the filtering work from O(n) per CD cycle to O(n) only when the data actually changes.

interviewQuestions.q: What does trackBy / track do in @for loops and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.a: Without track, Angular cannot identify which list item corresponds to which DOM node. On any data change (even if only one item changed), Angular destroys all DOM nodes and recreates them from scratch. With track item.id, Angular maintains a Map of id → DOM node. When the list updates, it only patches the specific nodes that changed, moves nodes that reordered, creates nodes for new items, and destroys nodes for removed items. For a list of 100 items where only 1 changed: without track = 100 DOM destroy + 100 DOM create operations; with track = 1 DOM patch. This becomes critical for large lists and frequent updates.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the core principles of a good Angular project structure?
interviewQuestions.a: Use feature-based folders (one folder per business domain/route) rather than type-based folders (all components together, all services together). Structure: core/ for singleton services, interceptors, guards, and layout components (provided once, used app-wide). features/ for business features — each feature owns its components, services, and models. shared/ for reusable UI components, pipes, and directives used by 2+ features. Dependencies flow one way: features can import from shared and core, but core and shared never import from features. Align feature folders with lazy-loaded routes for natural code splitting.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the most important TypeScript best practices in Angular?
interviewQuestions.a: Enable strict mode in tsconfig.json (strict: true) — this enables strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, and several other important checks. Never use any — it defeats TypeScript's purpose; use unknown with type guards, or proper generics. Use interfaces (not classes) for data shapes like API responses. Mark injected services as private readonly. Use TypeScript discriminated unions for async state (status: 'loading' | 'success' | 'error') instead of multiple boolean flags — this makes impossible states unrepresentable. Use explicit return types on public methods.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Angular protect against XSS and what can bypass those protections?
interviewQuestions.a: Angular automatically escapes all {{ interpolation }} and [property] bindings — HTML characters are converted to entities, so <script> tags render as text. [innerHTML] is automatically sanitised by Angular (removes script tags and event handlers). What bypasses protections: calling DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml() (only use for your own trusted SVG/HTML, never for user input), directly setting element.innerHTML in vanilla JS outside Angular's awareness, or using innerHTML in a Web Component. Other security practices: store JWT tokens in HttpOnly cookies or memory (not localStorage), validate input server-side, keep dependencies updated.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you implement lazy loading in Angular?
interviewQuestions.a: Use loadComponent() in route definitions with a dynamic import(): { path: 'admin', loadComponent: () => import('./features/admin/admin.component').then(m => m.AdminComponent) }. The import() call tells the build tool to split AdminComponent into a separate JS chunk. This chunk is only downloaded when the user navigates to /admin. The critical rule: never import the component class at the top of the routes file — a top-level import defeats lazy loading by including the class in the main bundle. Optionally add withPreloading(PreloadAllModules) to provideRouter() to download all chunks in the background after the main bundle loads.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Single Responsibility Principle and how does it apply to Angular components?
interviewQuestions.a: SRP says a class should have only one reason to change. For Angular components: a component should render one UI concept and handle events from it — nothing more. Signs a component violates SRP: it is over 300 lines, it injects 5+ services, it contains multiple unrelated sections of template or logic, it performs data fetching AND data display AND form handling AND navigation all in one. Fixes: extract repeated template blocks into sub-components, move business logic and HTTP calls to services, split large pages into a smart container with several focused dumb components.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a pure pipe and why is it preferred over component methods in templates?
interviewQuestions.a: A pure pipe (pure: true, the default) is a class decorated with @Pipe that Angular caches by its inputs. Angular only re-executes a pure pipe's transform() method when its inputs change — determined by reference equality (===). A component method is called on EVERY change detection cycle with no caching. For operations like filtering lists, formatting dates, or truncating text — which are called from templates — always prefer a pure pipe. The result: instead of recalculating a filtered 1000-item list on every mouse move, the pipe returns the cached result instantly until the list or filter value actually changes.
